data.title: Gold Marks A Double Top Despite Iran Conflict - Below $5,000 Soon?
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Gold bulls have long been waiting for geopolitical troubles to justify the metal's record prices.

However, markets often play rough games with high expectations, as we recently saw with Nvidia (NVDA) and Microsoft (MSFT) earnings. This aligns with the adage "Buy the rumor, sell the news."

Similar profit-taking moves have previously occurred in silver, platinum, and palladium, so investors might have thought gold would be exempt, given its reputation as a true safe haven.

However, the recent unexpected market behavior in relation to geopolitical conflict is surprising. Despite ongoing tensions and heavy military activity in the Middle East, stocks actually rallied after Trump assured the protection of the Strait of Hormuz.

Risk assets are decidedly rebounding, with cryptocurrencies surging to monthly highs as well as global stock markets; this proves how unusual these war flows have been.

So, why are safe havens selling off?

The US Department of Defense has certainly demonstrated its capabilities to rapidly damage key Iranian positions, alongside the Israeli Air Force.

Investors fear a prolonged conflict in the Middle East, so the idea of shorter operations is providing a sense of relief, as reflected in the market.

Investors also just received yet another report of US economic strength with another beat in Services PMI (56.1 vs 53.5 estimates), along with firming Private ADP Employment. Difficult to justify metal-boosting rate cuts with such solid data.

Friday will provide more clarity on that aspect with the monthly NFP data for February; expect this one to rock market expectations again!

Let’s conduct a multi-timeframe analysis of gold, as its recent price action has formed a double top. Will this signal the start of a real downtrend?

Gold (XAU/USD) Multi-timeframe analysis

Weekly Chart

Gold is now facing high-importance hurdles ahead.

After failing to regain its January $5,600 top despite the fundamental shift, sellers could now take the upper hand. This could particularly be the case when looking at the Weekly RSI forming a bearish divergence.

However, a counter-argument could maintain demand for Gold – Remaining above its Key weekly pivot zone, particularly above $5,100, proves that the action can still remain in balance on the higher timeframe.

Any break lower will also see streaks of support, with the most immediate major support a December highs around $4,550.

Gold Daily Chart

Gold sends out a more gloomy picture on the Daily timeframe, with the two recent bearish candles located at relative spikes forming a double-top.

While today's small rebound shows hesitancy, looking forward, as long as bulls can't manage to overtake the $5,379 highs from yesterday, technicals point to downside ahead.

Crossing back below $5,100 would allow sellers to re-take control of the short-term action, hinting at a retest of the mid-Feb lows ($4,844).

Breaking this level would then mark an official re-entry within the 2025 bull channel – $4,200 is its current lower bound (however it remains far from now).

Levels of interest for Gold trading:

Support Levels:

  • $5,100 Major Pivot turned support (Short-term: sellers in control below – Testing)
  • $4,850 to $4,900 Key Support (Mid-Feb Lows)
  • Pivotal Support and December record $4,400 to $4,500 (Bearish below)
  • Channel lows $4,200

Resistance Levels:

  • $5,250 Pivot Zone (+/- $50)
  • $5,400 Wartime Resistance
  • Current All-time Highs Resistance – $5,500 to $5,600

Gold 4H Chart

Gold is now rejecting its intraday key 50-period moving average acting as resistance.

A key test of the upward trendline will provide a last chance for bulls to re-take the short-term hand, before the double top materializes into a more consequential correction.

Watch for a break and 4H close below $5,100.

Safe Trades!

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Tracking Terry Smith's Fundsmith 13F Portfolio - Q4 2025 Update
data.description:
This article is part of a series that provides an ongoing analysis of the changes made to Fundsmith’s 13F portfolio on a quarterly basis. It is based on their regulatory 13F Form filed on 02/17/2026. Please visit our Tracking Terry Smith’s Fundsmith 13F Portfolio series to get an idea of their investment philosophy and our last update for the fund’s moves during Q3 2025.

This quarter, Fundsmith’s 13F portfolio value decreased from $19.80B to $17.12B. The number of holdings increased from 35 to 37. The top three holdings are at ~23% while the top five are close to ~38% of the 13F assets: Waters, Stryker, IDEXX, Visa, and Marriott International.

data.title: Every Metric Screams Buy - So Why Is Bitcoin Still Falling?
data.description:
Introduction: The depressing state of the crypto market

The overall crypto market has been a big disappointment to investors recently. Since Trump's victory in November 2024, positive headlines emerged such as the appointment of pro-crypto SEC chair Paul Atkins, approval of the Genius Act and increased institutional adoption.

If I told a reasonable investor about these positive developments beforehand, in November 2024, he would probably expect Bitcoin (BTC-USD) to be at all-time highs today. Most market participants did exactly that and made bullish Bitcoin forecasts (as shown in the table below).

Market Participant Bitcoin Forecasts (2026)
Citi $143,000
JP Morgan $170,000
Bernstein $150,000
VanEck $180,000
Average $160,750

Yet, Bitcoin is currently down 50% from its recent highs, back to its pre-election price level.

Best metrics show Bitcoin is Undervalued

The three charts below illustrate Bitcoin's oversold condition and signal strong recovery potential, including new all-time highs.

Bitcoin MVRV Z-Score

The Bitcoin MVRV Z-Score, which I first encountered through Lyn Alden, is, in my view, the single best indicator for gauging Bitcoin valuation. Here's how to interpret it.

The black line represents Bitcoin's market cap --- simply the price multiplied by the number of outstanding coins. The blue line represents Realized Value, which is the average price at which each Bitcoin was last transacted on-chain, multiplied by total circulating supply --- essentially the aggregate cost basis of all Bitcoin holders. The orange line is the Z-score, measuring how far market value deviates from realized value in standard deviations, and flagging periods of extreme overvaluation or undervaluation.

When the MVRV Z-Score is high, many holders are sitting on large unrealized profits, making it likely that long-term holders will begin distributing. When it is low, Bitcoin is historically oversold --- an opportune moment for investors to accumulate.

At present, the indicator is approaching its 2015, 2018, 2020, and 2022 lows. If you had bought Bitcoin in those periods, the forward one-year return was a whopping 184%.

Bitcoin/Gold Ratio

In my view, one of the worst feuds in the financial markets is the one between gold bugs and bitcoin enthusiasts. Both assets derive value from similar attributes, such as sound money traits, and both help investors protect their wealth against monetary debasement.

They have different strengths and weaknesses, of course. Gold has a strong track record going back 2,500 years, while Bitcoin allows for increased privacy and self-custody. Nevertheless, their shared characteristics indicate that they should move together depending on the macro backdrop.

Typically, when a prevailing market narrative creates a large outperformance of one or the other, it is a good time to buy. At current levels, Bitcoin buys only 12 ounces of gold, the lowest since 2023.

Note that since late 2023, the correlation between gold and Bitcoin has risen substantially, suggesting the market is increasingly recognizing Bitcoin's store-of-value properties - making the current selloff look like a temporary dislocation rather than a structural shift.

Liquidity

There are many ways to measure global liquidity, but for this analysis, we'll use US M2---a broad measure of money supply that includes physical currency, checking accounts, savings deposits, money market securities, and other forms of easily accessible cash.

Historically, Bitcoin's price has moved in lockstep with US M2 growth spikes, as the chart below clearly shows. Take the steady M2 climb from 2015-2018 under the Fed's near-zero rates, or the massive post-COVID money flood in 2020 to prop up the economy---both times were rocket fuel for BTC, turning liquidity into explosive gains.

Since the Fed peaked rates at 5.50% in mid-2023, liquidity conditions have turned decisively favorable. The Fed has cut ~175 bps to the current 3.50–3.75% range, with further easing expected throughout 2026. Meanwhile, unprecedented peacetime deficits (~6% of GDP) continue flooding the system with Treasury supply. While Bitcoin's response to liquidity sometimes lags, the relationship is grounded in sound fundamentals---prices will catch up.

The problem: Bitcoin caught in the middle of the Halo trade

As shown above, Bitcoin has seen increased institutional adoption, a positive change in the regulatory environment, while at the same time the main metrics scream a buying opportunity.

How on earth is the Bitcoin price still falling?

The problem is not with Bitcoin specifically, but with broad market sentiment. The S&P 500 YTD does not point to a bear market but to a major rotation. Investors are selling technology and high beta stocks and rotating to more defensive sectors like staples, energy, and materials.

The rotation is explained mainly by concerns about technology disruption.

With the rapid improvement of AI models, we are witnessing historic technology advancements; some argue they are even greater than the rise of the internet. Investors are trying to figure out which sectors will be disrupted and become obsolete. Forecasting complex third-order effects of technology developments is no easy task, so investors are making it simple: "sell whatever asset has even a remote chance of being disrupted and buy heavy assets, low-obsolescence companies, currently referred to as 'HALO'."

Bitcoin was caught in the middle of the crossfire, since it may be a victim of a nascent technology: Quantum Computing. If a sufficiently powerful quantum computer were developed, it could theoretically reverse-engineer private keys from public keys, compromising wallet security. Although I don't intend on becoming a quantum expert, I feel these concerns are overblown. Current quantum computers are nowhere near the estimated 4,000 logical qubits needed to threaten Bitcoin's encryption. Google's Willow, the most advanced quantum computer, operates at 105 qubits. The timeline for a credible threat is measured in decades, not years, and the Bitcoin developer community is already researching post-quantum cryptographic solutions.

Below, I attempt to show how Bitcoin has traded in line with disruptive technologies recently by comparing it to ARKK. ARKK is an actively managed ETF that concentrates on disruptive, growth companies favored by retail and momentum-driven investors.

A similar dynamic happened in the 2022 bear market. At first, Bitcoin sold-off together with disruptive stocks, as the market was concerned about technology stocks in general. Later in 2023, Bitcoin initiated a strong rally against the backdrop of BlackRock ETF filing in June 2023 and Fed pivot, outperforming ARKK by 41.6% over the period, even accounting for the recent drawdown.

I believe the same dynamic will play out this year. As the true impacts of technology disruption become clearer, investors will begin to separate the wheat from the chaff and Bitcoin will be bid. They will observe more carefully the increased institutional adoption, realize Bitcoin is here to stay and that it should actually be more correlated to gold than to technology stocks.

Risks

All of the main data points and frankly basic common-sense point to a much higher Bitcoin price one year from now. However, investing in Bitcoin always involves a degree of speculation and uncertainty.

Unforeseen events could push the price 20% lower, including a further escalation in the US-Iran conflict, a recession, or the bankruptcy of a large crypto player. A faster-than-expected advancement in quantum computing remains a tail risk worth monitoring.

Nevertheless, investing is not about certainties but about probabilities. And the probability of a higher bitcoin price is too great to ignore.

Taking into account a continuous improvement in liquidity conditions (M2), and assuming the Bitcoin/Gold ratio normalizes at 26 ounces, as the correlation of Bitcoin to tech assets breaks, I forecast a price of $140,000 in the next twelve months.

data.title: Paramount Skydance Stole The Show With Its Warner Bros. Discovery Purchase
data.description:
Last week, something happened that I was not expecting to happen. Back in early December of 2025, when news was circulating that Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) was getting close to choosing a buyer for its operations, I predicted that it was all but certain that Netflix (NFLX) would prevail. The other two leading bidders at that time were none other than Comcast Corporation (CMCSA) and Paramount Skydance (PSKY). But for different reasons, I viewed a combination involving those prospects as unlikely. In particular, I remember that while Paramount Skydance would strategically make the most sense because of its intention to absorb all of Warner Bros. Discovery, its ability to make a transaction of that size happen looked limited. By comparison, Netflix was a much more logical choice based on financial capability alone. After all, such a purchase, though certainly still large, would have been entirely achievable by the streaming giant.

Shortly after that, my prediction turned out to be right. Netflix was announced as the winner of the bid process, even though Paramount Skydance said that it had offered more. In another article, published in January of this year, I talked more about what the future looked like for the combined business. But after Paramount Skydance came out with an even better offer than it had provided previously, the picture changed entirely. You see, the final purchase price agreed upon between Netflix and Warner Bros. Discovery saw Netflix agreeing to pay $27.75 per share in cash for Warner Bros. Discovery. This agreement was later amended to also include proceeds from the spin-off of some of the assets that Warner Bros. Discovery owned that it called Discovery Global. But even after all of that was factored in, Paramount Skydance persisted, ultimately agreeing to buy Warner Bros. Discovery in an all cash deal valued at $31 per share.

This is most certainly an interesting transaction. And it is only possible thanks to a large amount of outside funding. If things go according to plan, the end result will be an entertainment behemoth that has the potential to thrive in the streaming and movie production industries. But for shareholders of Paramount Skydance, there are certain risks involved with this transaction. Namely, there is considerable uncertainty about proposed synergies. But if those do come to fruition, there could be some upside for shareholders of the business.

A path I didn’t expect

On February 27th of this year, news broke that Paramount Skydance struck a deal to acquire Warner Bros. Discovery in an all cash deal valued at $31 per share. This is a massive transaction, with an enterprise value of about $110.1 billion based on my own calculations. At first glance, that purchase price might seem absurd considering that Paramount Skydance has an enterprise value on its own of only $26.3 billion. And a good chunk of that, about $10.4 billion, is in the form of net debt. Typically speaking, a purchase of this magnitude would not be feasible. Even taking on tremendous amounts of debt would leave the business over leveraged. However, management made something magical work. And that was the entrance of one of the wealthiest men in the world.

David Ellison, Chairman and CEO of Paramount Skydance, happens to be the son of Larry Ellison, who served as the cofounder of Oracle (ORCL). Today, the elder Ellison is worth an estimated $197 billion, making him one of the wealthiest people on the planet. Presumably with the backing of Larry Ellison, the Ellison family has agreed to put in $47 billion in cash in order to make this deal happen. To be clear, they aren't alone in this contribution. They have partnered up with RedBird Capital Partners, which is a private investment firm that invests significant amounts of capital into media opportunities, sports opportunities, the general entertainment space, and even certain technology firms.

The structure of the transaction is relatively straightforward. The $47 billion will go toward stock in Paramount Skydance at a price of $16.02 per share. This will result in the issuance of about 2.9 billion shares, taking the total share count for Paramount Skydance up to about 4 billion. The transaction will also see new debt issued of about $38.6 billion, in addition to a bridge commitment aimed at refinancing some of Warner Bros. Discovery’s debt in the amount of $15 billion. There are other components to the transaction as well, such as rolling over existing debt. And on top of this, there's going to have to be another $2.8 billion in cash taken from the balance sheet to cover the breakup fee payable to Netflix over this maneuver. Without that breakup fee, we get an enterprise value of $110.1 billion, with the market capitalization side of the transaction amounting to $80.9 billion. It is worth noting that existing shareholders of Paramount Skydance will receive the opportunity to buy shares at the same price that the Ellison family and RedBird are paying through an equity rights offering that will occur close to the date of the close of the transaction. That would amount to as much as $3.25 billion.

The fact that this is being done in the form of equity means that leverage is a lot less than it otherwise would be. Based on my own calculations, net debt of the combined business will be about $79.8 billion. And the enterprise value should stand at about $139.2 billion. Overall revenue from this business should be somewhere in the range of $69 billion. That's based on estimates for 2026. To put this in perspective, Netflix generated $45.2 billion in sales last year. So while there are legitimate operational differences between Netflix and what Paramount Skydance combined with Warner Bros. Discovery will do, this illustrates for you just how large of a player in the entertainment space we are talking about.

In my article from last year where I predicted that Netflix would be the buyer, I provided significant detail into the positives and negatives of the other prospective purchasers. That included Paramount Skydance. So I don't think it would make a great deal of sense to go through those details again when you can just read that article. On a combined basis, what we do know is that the assets that generate this $69 billion in revenue will be tremendous. For instance, the combined streaming services of this giant will have more than 200 million global subscribers. Only two streaming companies have subscriber numbers this large or larger. One of them is Netflix, which has an estimated 325 million global subscribers. The other is The Walt Disney Company (DIS). Between Disney+ and Hulu, it boasted 195.7 million subscribers at the end of its latest fiscal year. But that does not include ESPN+, which has around 24.1 million subscribers to bring the total to about 219.8 million.

Although the linear portfolio the company is destined for long term decline, it is still rather significant in that space, serving more than 200 countries and territories across the globe. It serves as a distributor of major US and international sports, including the NFL and the Olympics. And then we also have a tremendous content library that includes things like Harry Potter, Game of Thrones, and The Lord of the Rings. Management is committing to more than 30 theatrical projects every year. To put this in perspective, Disney collected box office revenue from only 16 movies last year. And of these, only 10 of them came out during 2025.

If everything goes according to plan, combining Paramount Skydance and Warner Bros. Discovery opens the door to significant cost savings. On a combined basis for 2026, the $69 billion in revenue generated by the company should yield EBITDA of about $12 billion. But when you add on top of this $6 billion in expected synergies, that brings us up to $18 billion. This alone should have a positive impact on our overall average. You see, the close of the transaction, the combined enterprise should have a net leverage ratio of about 6.5. But if we factor in the synergies, this will drop to 4.3. And within three years of the closing of the transaction, a rise in profitability combined with what is almost certain to be some debt reduction, should push this ratio down to approximately 3. Add on top of this the prospect of a mid single digit revenue growth rate for the 2026 through 2030 fiscal years, and it's not unreasonable to be cautiously optimistic about where we are.

These cost savings will come from some difficult choices that management has to make. Examples here include selling off some of its real estate, and reducing corporate overhead. But other cost savings will come from productivity initiatives. This includes procurement efficiencies that management is targeting, benefits associated with consolidating streaming technology stacks, improvements when it comes to marketing, with the company optimizing spending on agencies and tooling, and integrating ERP and certain other corporate IT systems.

Whether or not this transaction ends up being attractive will depend a tremendous amount on whether these synergies are ultimately captured. In the chart below, for instance, you can see how, using historical results for 2025, both Paramount Skydance and Warner Bros. Discovery happened to be valued. I also calculated valuations based on figures from 2024. The important thing to notice is that Warner Bros. Discovery is quite a bit more expensive than its suitor. But in that same chart, you can see how the combined company shapes up from a valuation standpoint, both with synergies and without them. Without synergies, there is cause for concern. And this primarily stems from the fact that it would mean that investors in the remaining company, Paramount Skydance will ultimately be paying more for the cash flows that the company generates compared to if the transaction had never occurred. But with synergies, we come in striking distance of what Paramount Skydance is worth.

Takeaway

The way I see it, the picture right now for shareholders of Paramount Skydance and Warner Bros. Discovery is uncertain, but interesting. Netflix missing out on this transaction might be disappointing to some. But for those who are believers in the transaction between Paramount Skydance and Warner Bros. Discovery, while risk does exist, opportunity does also if things go right. As a value investor, I am not a big fan of uncertainty. What I do see is that while there is potential, that hinges on management's ability to aggressively cut costs. And until we see that such maneuvers can materialize, I think that the best approach is to rate both enterprises a ‘hold’.

data.title: Geopolitical Tensions Boost Palantir's Bull Case
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Although Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR) has fallen significantly from its all-time highs of $207.5 and since my last coverage, the stock is at a very attractive entry point, as the fundamental growth story in AI and defense remains firmly in place.

Specifically, due to rising tensions in Eastern Europe and the Middle East, governments are forced to increase their defense spending. This is a perfect environment for a company to take massive data sets and turn them into actionable intelligence in real-time. As such, PLTR is in a perfect position to land more government contracts and increase their relationship with NATO and the U.S. defense establishment.

Palantir Targets 61% Growth, 50%+ Margins in 2026

In my view, Palantir has a solid growth in the U.S. commercial sector that serves as its main performance factor for financials. This segment is growing and the rate of expansion is compounding. Q4 results indicate that U.S. commercial topline increased by 137% YoY. This YoY growth exceeds Q3's YoY growth of 121% and the Q2's 93% growth. This QoQ acceleration proves that Palantir is capturing market share at an increasing pace instead of seeing demand plateau. The factor behind this acceleration is the AI Platform (AIP).

Palantir has sharply converted interest in gen AI into future top line through bootcamps. As per Palantir’s management, clients are moving from initial engagement to production usage quickly. One utility client increased from an annualized contract value of $7 million to $31 million by the end of 2025.

Likewise, one energy client increased from $4 million to more than $20 million. These instances indicate that once Palantir installs its OS, the client consumption scales largely. This land and expansion factor led to a Net Dollar Retention rate of 139%. It is a 5%-point increase from Q3. The growth in this metric points that existing clients are spending progressively more money with Palantir.

Moreover, I see, this top line expansion happens without hurting profitability. In my experience, for many software comps, high growth comes with high cash burn. But, Palantir does not follow this trend. Its Rule of 40 score of 127% has increased by 46%-points YoY. Palantir derived $798 million in adjusted operating income in Q4. For FY25, adjusted FCF hit $2.27 billion and it is representing a 51% margin. This indicates that for every dollar of top line Palantir brings in, ~51 cents converts to FCF.

This capital efficiency supports Palantir with a cash pile of $7.2 billion that is securing its balance sheet against economic shocks. The U.S. government sector supports this commercial expansion. Government topline grew 66% YoY in Q4. Palantir secured a contract worth up to $448 million with the U.S. Navy for ShipOS. It targets modernizing shipbuilding supply chains.

So, I think Palantir's software is entrenching itself in critical gov infrastructure. This is making displacement by competitors hard over time. The combined momentum of U.S. commercial and government sectors led in Palantir closing 180 deals valued at least $1 million and 61 deals valued over $10 million in Q4.

Further, Palantir’s guidance indicates this trend may continue with FY26 topline between $7.182 billion and $7.198 billion. This is a 61% YoY growth rate. Palantir expects U.S. commercial top line to exceed $3.144 billion in FY26 this means a growth rate of ~115%. With that, I believe there is no saturation in the U.S. market. Its high top line growth with high FCF margins forms a profile as Palantir can self-fund product development and expansion without needing external financing. The rapid migration of clients from trial phases to large-scale contracts points to the utility of the AIP and the Ontology system.

As clients like Lear Corporation (LEA) go from 100 users to 16,000 users, Palantir may continue to secure long-term recurring top line. This operational leverage means Palantir can add top line faster than it adds costs, pushing margins higher. The 2026 guidance for adjusted operating income is between $4.126 billion-$4.142 billion. This means to me that Palantir is stabilizing bottom line.

Palantir’s Valuation Leaves No Margin for Error

For me, Palantir still has large risk on its valuation multiples as it topline concentration on a single geographic market. A disconnection between Palantir's stock price and standard industry valuation metrics continues. Palantir stock trades at a P/E non-GAAP ratio of 109x on a forward basis. The sector median is ~22x. This means Palantir trades at a premium of 400% compared to its market.

Further, the forward P/S ratio is at ~47x, whereas the sector median is only 3.1x. This 1,442% difference indicates that the market has priced Palantir for extreme perfection. This high valuation leaves zero margin for error. If Palantir misses growth targets by even a small fraction, the multiple compression can be very high. This elevated pricing is a setup where even strong financial results may not satisfy Street expectations if the rate of acceleration slows.

The source of this risk is the extreme top line concentration of growth within the U.S. Palantir's performance is lopsided, as in Q4, U.S. commercial revenue grew 137% YoY against international commercial topline growth of 8%. For FY25, international commercial topline grew a mere 2% and this indicates that Palantir may not replicate its U.S. lead abroad.

As per CEO Alexander Karp, Palantir does not have the bandwidth to address issues outside of America. There is a hesitance to adopt elements for its products in Europe and Canada. This geographic limitation restricts Palantir's TAM in the short-term. If the U.S. economy faces a downturn/if U.S. enterprise spending tightens, Palantir lacks a diversified international top line to buffer the impact.

The international business is stagnant against the domestic business. With the U.S. top line now comprising 77% of total revenue, Palantir is exposed to the single-market. In my view, the hesitance in Europe is not a sales problem. It is an issue regarding how those markets purchase and implement software. European institutions may prefer domestic products and lack the structure to bear the load of buying advanced OSs like Palantir's.

Further, the Price-to-Book ratio is 30x against a sector median of 4.35x. It means Street is paying 30x the value of Palantir's net assets. I see high stock-based compensation (SBC) also a problem. In Q4, Palantir spent $196 million in stock-based compensation expenses. For FY25, this expense was $684 million. Although Palantir is GAAP profitable, this high level of SBC dilutes current stockholders.

The lack of international scaling capability is a long-term risk to the financials required to justify the high P/E ratio. To maintain such a multiple, Palantir may need a global expansion. However, Palantir's management is focusing almost exclusively on the U.S. due to resource and market readiness. This means that the international lag will continue. If the U.S. market saturates, Palantir will hit a growth wall as it has not created the international relationships/product market fit needed to stabilize its current growth rates.

Overall, depending mostly on U.S. enterprises’ adoption to support a $300+ billion market cap is a high-risk case for forward financials. Any regulatory problem and economic shift in the U.S. market may have an outsized negative impact on Palantir's stock price under the lack of international support.

Takeaway

Although PLTR stock has corrected significantly from its highs, the long-term story for the stock holds good as it continues to tap into the hypergrowth potential of AI for growth in the defense and space industries with high profitability and cash flow generation. Despite the high valuation and domestic concentration risks, the stock continues to win contracts and tap into AI, which keeps the high valuation multiple for the stock valid.

data.title: Chasing The Catch-Up: Why The Market Is Wrong To Reward Entegris
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome The Straits Strategist as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Investment Thesis

Rating: SELL

After years of share price underperformance, Entegris' share price has rallied 50% YTD and is currently trading at 40x its FY 2026 P/E. The Street views Entegris as an AI beneficiary and has assigned the company a hefty multiple similar to other SPE stocks. In my opinion however, Entegris is a SELL based on a few key reasons.

First, Entegris is seeing very little benefit from AI applications, only 5% of wafer starts are derived from AI applications. Second, we all know that the electronics industry is facing a slowdown due to the current memory shortage, this will weigh down on overall industry wafer starts which will be negative for the stock. Third, the rising competition that the firm is facing in China will also threaten the group's China-based revenue. And lastly, Entegris is still burdened with debt from its CMC Materials acquisition, which will limit the company's potential to distribute dividends and initiate share buybacks.

Introduction

If you don't know Entegris, this is a key supplier of advanced materials and process solutions for the semiconductor industry. While companies like NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Corporation Limited (TSM) and Intel Corporation (INTC) get all the fame, Entegris is the key backbone that makes their product possible, as they provide the necessary chemistry and infrastructure that allows for the fabrication of modern semiconductors.

Its business can be divided into two main areas:

Materials Solutions

46% of the group's revenue comes from this segment. This segment was born from the company's acquisition of CMC Materials. Most of the products are consumables used in semiconductor equipment, such as CMP Slurries, CMP Pads and Advanced Deposition Materials. These chemicals are constantly being used during the fabrication process, and has to be consistently restocked, which creates a stream of recurring revenue for the company.

Advanced Purity Solutions

54% of the group's revenue comes from this segment. Most of the products here are focused on improving the yield of semiconductors. During the fabrication process, micro-particles can contaminate the wafers which will increase the defect rate. Here, Entegris' filtration and purification system works by removing most micro-particles from the cleanroom, which is why the world's leading foundries such as Taiwan Semiconductor require the use of Entegris' products.

Figure 1: Segmental Breakdown of Entegris Revenue

Entegris as an AI Beneficiary

The proliferation of AI has uplifted the entire semiconductor industry, Entegris has also benefited from this progression, and management is repeatedly emphasizing the company's status as an AI beneficiary. The two key arguments that I've observed through the company's earnings call is that:

(1) Advanced Process Node Migration: AI applications often requires the use of advanced process nodes, such as Nvidia's Blackwell which uses 4nm, and AMD's MI350 which uses 3nm. In the context of Entegris, this migration is favorable to them, as cutting-edge chips requires a lot more content per wafer. Although these processes are not the leading-edge (which is currently Taiwan Semiconductor's 2nm), they are nonetheless a key driver for the overall migration process.

(2) Driving Wafer Start Growth: Entegris is mainly a unit-driven business (75% of their revenue is unit-driven). In this case, unit-driven means that their products are consumables which are highly dependent on the total of wafer volumes shipped by the industry. This metric can be measured in MSI — Millions of Square Inches; The higher the industry MSI is, the higher the implied growth of Entegris, and vice versa. What has happened is that over the past few years, industry MSI has stagnated, which has resulted in Entegris underperforming in terms of delivering financial results. However, this has changed with the advent of AI as AI-driven demand for logic and memory is pushing for MSI to restart growth in 2026. Management is currently optimistic on this fact as they are guiding for mid-single digit growth in MSI for the year.

Figure 2: Material Spend per Wafer in Logic and 3D NAND

How are Investors Reacting? The market is clearly optimistic about the potential rebound of Entegris. Its share price has rallied more than 30% YTD as of writing, which is a clear proof of this. Currently, the street is viewing Entegris as a catch-up play. Previously, their share price has significantly underperformed its WFE peers and the broader market over the past two years. This has happened even in spite of the fact that the broader industry had experienced a massive surge in growth due to AI applications. With the restart of industry MSI growth, the street is looking for the situation to reverse.

Figure 3: Entegris Share Price Performance Year-To-Date (YTD), Source: Google.

Why I am not excited about Entegris AI Opportunity

AI has a marginal impact on overall MSI growth

While the growth for AI chips is showing no signs of slowing down, this has clearly not driven the growth for overall wafer starts. AI-driven wafers starts only account for a very small portion of industry wafer starts. According to management, only 5% of wafer starts can be attributed to AI application. In my view, this has explained why Entegris has lagged behind despite of the 2024 and 2025 AI boom. If Entegris was truly an AI beneficiary, they clearly should have seen MSI growth starting way earlier, and it raises the question of why are they only seeing it now? This 5% is insufficient to move the needle for the stock. Given the fact that they are many other options available to us as investors to gain AI exposure, this is a key reason why Entegris is not an attractive pick.

Figure 4: Industry MSI Shipments and Wafer Revenue

Another fact is that Entegris is only deriving a small portion of their revenue from the migration of leading-edge nodes. Management has previously quoted that 40% of revenue is derived from advanced process nodes (defined as 7nm and below). In my initial analysis, I also thought that this was referring to 40% of the group's overall revenue. But after digging further, this actually refers to 40% of Fab-related revenue, which is in-turn 60% of the group's overall revenue. The actual leading-node contribution is at 40% x 60% = 24% of the total group's revenue, which is much less than expected. This is also why I do not prefer Entegris as a beneficiary of advanced node migration, as it only accounts for a small fraction of revenue.

Figure 5: Entegris Exposure by Customer and Application

Headwinds from Memory Shortage and China Localization

Management has largely emphasized on its status as an AI beneficiary, but they have ignored highlighting on the developments of the broader electronics sector. Most of industry MSI volumes is still coming from traditional applications, such as smartphones, automobiles, PCs and other consumer electronics. These applications are facing a severe crisis from the rise in memory prices, which can be felt through two ways. One is that the situation will increase cost and force price hikes for consumer electronics, which will reduce the overall unit demand. Another is that consumer electronics brands may face a shortage in memory supply which could limit their unit production. With the majority of smartphones and PC brands forecasted to lower production for 2026, this is expected to hurt overall wafer starts.

Figure 6: IDC Forecast for Smartphone and PC Shipment Volumes

Another headwind is that Entegris is seeing strong headwinds in the Chinese market. This will also offset the benefit that the company is seeing from AI. With the large geopolitical uncertainty at the global stage, many Chinese foundries are looking to source more of their equipment and material from local Chinese vendors. This will be harmful to Entegris as a native US firm. The key competitors of Entegris in China are Anji Microelectronics for CMP slurries and Cobetter Filtration for filtration systems. I expect that these firms will be able to gradually take more share from Entegris in the upcoming years due to this trend. As of FY2026, 20% of Entegris revenue is derived from China, and two-thirds of this category is from domestic Chinese firms, which are the most at-risk group.

Figure 7: Entegris 2025 Geographic Segmentation

Entegris is still Debt-Burdened from its CMC Materials Acquisition

Last on our list, is that Entegris is still paying the price for its expensive acquisition of CMC Materials in 2022 (acquired for $6.5 billion). CMC Material, like Entegris, is a critical supplier of semiconductor materials. Since the acquisition, CMC Materials has been consolidated into the group as the Material Solutions division. The main rationale of management for the acquisition was that Entegris could bundle CMC Material's products together with its own to increase the content per wafer. I agree that the acquisition has boosted Entegris' competitive position, but it came at the cost of significantly expanding its debt levels.

From 2021 to 2022, long-term debts of Entegris had spiked from $937 million to more than $5.6 billion post-acquisition. To combat this massive spike in debt, management has divested several non-cash businesses to repay its debts. However, a debt of more than $3.7 billion still remains on the balance sheet. Meanwhile, most of Entegris' $396 million of FCF has to be dedicated to servicing this debt. In this case, I think that very little can be expected from investors in terms of shareholder rewards (dividends and buybacks). From an investors point of view, this will be unappealing given that we would have to wait a long period to receive rewards from managements.

Figure 8: Entegris Balance Sheet from 2021 to 2025

Financial Summary

Now moving on to Entegris's financials, starting off with profitability metrics, Entegris's margins has been resilient over the past few years. Gross margins has remained stable at between 42% to 46% despite the cyclical nature of the industry. This tells me that product cost is very predictable for the company. Operating profit margins has also remained stable at between 14% to 16% post CMC Materials acquisition. I think that this metric has room for improvement, with management having recently completed the expansion of its Taiwan and Colorado plants. We will be able to observe this especially as the company steadily ramps up production at these facilities.

In terms of return-related metrics, Entegris is underperforming in terms of this area. For both ROA and ROE, ROA has hovered at a measly 2% to 3%, which is not satisfactory. This tells me that the company may not be earning a sufficient rate of return to cover the cost of capital from both shareholders and debt holders. ROE performing at a higher level of between 5% to 8%, but given the firm's increase in leverage, this could be the result of the debt-fueled acquisition. Actually, for Entegris' prior to the acquisition, these metrics had performed at a much more satisfactory level. The acquisition had greatly reduced both ROA and ROE, which is also another reason why I feel that the acquisition came at too high of a cost for the company.

Figure 9: Entegris Financial Ratios from 2021 to 2025

Valuation

The main driver of Entegris’ earnings will be its operating leverage; although the Street expects only single-digit growth in its topline, earnings are projected to grow at a rate of 20%+. In terms of forward P/E, Entegris is currently trading at around 40x FY2026 P/E, which is relatively expensive given that EPS is projected to grow at 24% for the year. This would place Entegris at a PEG ratio of roughly 1.7, which is above the typically recommended level for this metric.

Figure 10: Analyst Estimates for Entegris 2026 & 2027, Source: Quartr (Data by S&P Global)

However, relative to its peers, Entegris seems to be trading at a more reasonable level. Compared to the Big Four WFE manufacturers, Entegris trades at a similar P/E multiple (with those companies priced around 35x–46x P/E). However, given that Entegris is growing faster than most of these peers, it is relatively cheap on a PEG basis. This contrasts with the absolute numbers, where Entegris’s valuation looks expensive on a standalone basis.

Figure 11: Relative Valuation between Entegris and WFE Peers, from S&P Global and Author's Calculations

Risk to the Recommendation

Considering that the recommendation is to SELL the stock, there are a few potential upside risks that could act against the SELL recommendation:

Better than Expected Industry MSI Starts

While I expect that MSI will be affected by the stagnation of traditional applications, there could be potential upside if the impact of the slowdown is minimal.

Faster than Expected Debt Repayment

While Entegris’ large outstanding debt is expected to weigh down the company's ability to reward shareholders, any faster-than-expected debt repayment would allow the company to reward shareholders sooner than expected.

Stronger than Expected AI Contribution

While only 5% of wafer starts are currently from AI applications, if this figure eventually reaches a substantial level, it could pose a potential upside risk.

Conclusion

There is no doubt that Entegris plays a critical role in the semiconductor ecosystem. Without Entegris, commercially viable semiconductors would not be possible. However, given that management has largely discounted the impact of the slowdown in traditional applications, this could be a strong downside catalyst when the primary impacts begin to materialize going into 2026. Furthermore, the threat the company is facing from the China localization trend may offset the upside that AI poses to the company. The lack of shareholder rewards due to the company's focus on debt repayment is also another reason why I am not bullish on the stock. Although Entegris appears cheap on a relative valuation basis, its expensive 40x P/E multiple is why I think Entegris is not attractive to accumulate at this juncture. The stock would have been much more attractive at the beginning of the year, prior to the 50% YTD rally. Hence, I initiate coverage on the stock with a SELL recommendation.

data.title: Bank of England Set For March Pause But Don't Write Off Further Cuts
data.description:
By James Smith, Developed Markets Economist, UK

Inflation could test 3.5% if energy prices stay elevated into Q2

Investors have slashed expectations for a March rate cut from the Bank of England; markets are pricing it with just a 20% probability, down from 80% pre-conflict.

We are pushing back our call for the next cut to April, though we wouldn’t rule out a move this month. Remember, those who have voted for rate cuts at recent meetings have done so because the labour market is getting weaker. That hasn’t changed.

Still, the Bank of England has shown itself to be particularly sensitive to supply-driven spikes in headline inflation, more so than the Federal Reserve or the European Central Bank. Last summer’s hawkish response to higher food prices made that abundantly clear. Chief Economist Huw Pill has often cited 3.5-4% as a level for headline CPI, which, if reached, is statistically much more likely to morph into a longer-lasting bout of price pressure.

That threshold could easily be tested if natural gas prices stay at or above 120p/therm - equivalent to 50 EUR/MWh on the Dutch TTF benchmark - into Q2 and if oil prices persistently flirt with 85-90 USD/bbl. Headline inflation would peak at 3.5% in late summer. And that’s before considering the secondary impact on food and services inflation. A more extreme scenario where oil prices go to 110 USD/bbl and European gas prices rise persistently above 65 EUR/MWh could push headline inflation temporarily close to 5%.

We estimate every 10% rise in oil prices adds 0.1ppt to headline inflation. Every 10% rise in gas prices is worth 0.15ppt.

Three scenarios for UK headline inflation (YoY%)

Source: Macrobond, ING

Cooler jobs market will limit the impact on wage growth and services inflation

But this will take some time to show through fully. Household energy prices are capped by the regulator, and only 40% of that cap is directly affected by gas prices. The next update isn’t until July, and that’ll be based on average wholesale prices from mid-February to mid-May, focusing on energy contracts for delivery next autumn/winter. That means the more short-lived the energy price spike, the less of an impact it will have on households this summer.

Rising energy prices inevitably revive memories of the 2022 shock. But there’s an important difference: the jobs market is much, much weaker now. In early 2022, more than half of firms told the Bank it was “much harder” than usual to recruit. Today that figure is just 10%. Vacancies are substantially lower and unemployment higher, meaning workers have less bargaining power to drive up wage growth and protect their disposable incomes.

Something similar is true of corporate pricing power. Food retailers wouldn’t struggle to pass on higher costs, but consumer services probably will. They were a key source of inflation amid the 2022 spike; energy is a key input cost for hospitality and others. Those same industries have been shedding workers through 2025. Further cost pressure from energy bills is more likely to trigger even lower headcounts than higher prices, limiting the impact on services inflation relative to 2022.

Much also depends on how the government reacts. In 2022, policies including a £2,500 cap on household energy bills cost £50bn - or 2% of GDP. That kept the UK economy out of recession and arguably provoked a more significant rate-hiking cycle than might have otherwise come to pass. Temporarily cutting VAT on energy bills, lowering fuel duty, and transferring some of the cost of household energy bills onto general taxation are obvious levers if the crisis escalates.

Yet the Treasury is constrained by its fiscal rules. Yesterday’s Spring Statement may have shown the government has ample “headroom” under those rules, but that starts to disappear when you consider mounting spending pressures - not least from defence - that are likely to come into sharper focus this year. Though gilt issuance is falling back, markets would not take kindly to an unfunded energy support package, potentially tying the Treasury’s hands on how much support it can offer without countervailing tax increases.

In short, we still think UK interest rates have further to fall. But if energy prices stay at or above current levels, it’s hard to see the Bank cutting rates in March. It has previously said decisions are becoming more finely balanced as we approach the neutral level of interest rates. And remember that while the committee is heavily divided, Governor Andrew Bailey de facto has the deciding vote. Though he has leant into the idea of a March cut, it wouldn’t take much for him to vote for no change again this month.

We’ve pushed back our call for the next cut to April - though a significant de-escalation over the next few weeks could yet bring a March move back onto the table.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more
