data.title: Iovance Biotherapeutics: Short-Term Pain, Long-Term Gain - I'm Still Bullish After All These Years
data.description:
Investment Overview

I have been covering Iovance Biotherapeutics (IOVA) in notes for Seeking Alpha since 2020, when I initiated coverage of the San Carlos, California headquartered biotech / pharma company with a Buy rating. Sadly, shares traded at a price of $35 then, and today they trade at just $3.75 - down nearly 90% - valuing the company at a market cap of $1.54bn.

In some ways, that's surprising, as the core focus of the company has always been its tumor infiltrating lymphocyte ("TIL") therapy lifileucel, and in 2020, lifileucel had just entered a pivotal Phase 2 clinical trial treating patients with advanced melanoma (skin cancer), and the company was still working on the construction of a manufacturing facility in Philadelphia.

Today, lifileucel, brand name Amtagvi, is a commercially available therapy for advanced melanoma, having been approved in February 2024, and together with Proleukin (aldesleukin), an interleukin-2, or IL-2, product used in the Amtagvi, treatment regimen, helped Iovance post a revenue figure of $264m in 2025. Meanwhile, the Iovance Cell Therapy Center ("iCTC") in Philadelphia, is the first FDA-approved facility to manufacture commercial TIL cell therapy (source: Iovance 10K filing 2025).

Management has consistently stated that it expects Amtagvi to become a "blockbuster" (>$1bn revenues per annum) product in advanced melanoma treatment in the US alone, but is also targeting an approval in second line non-small cell lung cancer ("NSCLC") treatment, a market management believes is seven times larger than melanoma. Amtagvi has also been approved in Canada, and could secure approvals in the UK, EU, Switzerland and Australia within the next two years.

That being the case, we may ask why Iovance's market cap valuation is so low? There are several reasons for this, in my view.

Firstly, it took far longer than expected to secure approval for Amtagvi than initially thought - the original biologics license application ("BLA") requesting FDA approval of the drug was submitted in 2022, but approval not granted until 2024, testing Wall Street's patience.

Secondly, Iovance is heavily loss making company - as we can see in the table below, expenses have far outstripped revenues.

Thirdly, commercially speaking Amtagvi has underperformed both analysts and management's own expectations - Iovance had initially guided for revenues of $450m-$475m in 2025, but slashed that figure to $250m-$300m in May last year, causing its share price to sink from ~$3.6 per share, to ~$1.6 per share.

Fourthly, treatment with Amtagvi is a lengthy process that can be hard on patients - a small piece of tumor tissue must be removed from the patient, possibly requiring an outpatient procedure, T-cells are separated from the sample at Iovance's manufacturing facility, grown into the billions of cells, shipped back to the treatment centre and administered to the patient, who must undergo a course of lymphodepletion chemotherapy before receiving the cells, and a course of therapy with Proleukin afterwards. As I noted in my last post on Iovance in December:

Concerns have been raised around Amtagvi's safety record - mortality rate in its pivotal melanoma study stood at 7.5%, including 2 deaths during the lymphodepleting period, 6 deaths within 30 days, and 4 deaths 38 to 150 days following AMTAGVI administration.

Fifthly, Amtagvi is an expensive therapy, that launched with a list price of >$500k, and finally, competition in the TIL and melanoma, lung, and other solid tumor cancer markets is intense Iovance discusses this in its 2025 annual report / 10K filing as below:

our TIL cell therapies face significant competition from multiple companies such as BioNtech (BNTX), Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY), Daiichi Sankyo, Eisai, Genmab (GMAB), Immunocore (IMCR), IO Biotech, Merck (MRK), Moderna (MRNA), Pfizer (PFE), Regeneron Pharmaceuticals (REGN), and Replimune (REPL).

AbelZeta Pharma, Alaunos Therapeutics (TCRT), Biosyngen, GRIT Biotechnology, Immatics (IMTX), Immunocore (IMCR), Intima Bioscience, KSQ Therapeutics, Marker Therapeutics (MRKR), Obsidian Therapeutics, and others are developing T cell therapies based on genetically engineered T cell receptors rendered reactive against tumor-associated antigens, other genetically engineered TIL products, and TIL products designed to be reactive to specific neoantigens.

In summary, Iovance is often considered a polarising investment opportunity. Clearly, long-term investors have suffered significant losses, but with that said, since I issued a Sell rating on stock in April 2025, my subsequent three updates, in July, October, and December, have all been Buy ratings, and shares are up respectively 68%, 78%, and 31% since.

Given Iovance released its Q4 and full-year 2025 earnings and business updates on 25th February, and with multiple key catalysts in play in 2026, I feel it's time to re-evaluate the Buy thesis once again to see if recent developments have strengthened or undermine, so here goes. Let's start with an overview of the 2025 earnings.

Iovance - 2025 Earnings Overview - A Familiar Mixture Of Pros and Cons

As mentioned, Iovance earned $264m of revenues in 2025, which met management's (downgraded) guidance - I had noted in my last post in December:

Iovance has promised a strong Q4 - to meet its revenue guidance for 2025, the company will need to earn at least ~$75m, and to meet the higher end, ~$123m.

Fourth quarter revenues amounted to ~$87m in the end, with Amtagvi earning ~$65m, and Proleukin, ~$22m. Gross margin achieved was ~50%, which management stated reflected "increasing benefits from solid execution and cost optimization".

Net loss in Q4 was $(72m), compared to $(78.6m) in the prior year quarter, with total costs and expenses of $160m being almost identical to Q4 2024. For the full-year, Amtagvi earned revenues of ~$220m, and Proleukin ~$44m, while total costs and expenses amounted to $667m, compared to $559m in 2024. Loss from operations amounted to $(403m), and net loss $(391m), or $(1.09) per share, compared to $(372m), or $(1.28) per share in 2024.

Cash and equivalents was reported by the company of $297m, with $6m of restricted cash. The company reported no long-term debt, but it seems clear that Iovance cannot afford to make a loss in 2026 comparable to 2025, or the funding runway may be exhausted. Management stated on its Q3 earnings call that:

We successfully extended our cash position to fund operations into the third quarter of 2027

In a real world setting, Amtagvi is looking like an effective therapy - management reported in its earnings press release that:

An oral presentation at the 2026 Tandem Meetings of the American Society for Transplantation and Cellular Therapy (ASTCT®) and the Center for International Blood and Marrow Transplant Research (CIBMTR®) reported:

  • An unprecedented overall objective response rate ("ORR") of ~44%.
  • Higher ORR of 52% after two or fewer prior lines of therapy, highlighting the importance of early treatment.

Five-year analysis of the pivotal study of Amtagvi in melanoma patients revealed an ORR of 31.4%, five-year overall survival ("OS") of 19.7%, median duration of response ("mDor" of 36.5 months, and median OS of 13.9 months.

Iovance further noted a "continuously growing and maturing network of U.S. authorized treatment centers ("ATCs"), including Academic and community ATCs, as well as the opening of a specialty pharmacy distribution channel, and that manufacturing turnaround improved to 32 days or less from inbound to return shipment to ATCs.

Approvals in the UK and Australia are apparently achievable in 2026, although Switzerland approval is not expected until 2027, and Iovance says it is working with the European Medicines Agency ("EMA") to resubmit a marketing authorisation application ("MAA") there - the original submission was withdrawn owing to a lack of alignment around clinical data required to support a submission.

Management declined to provide specific financial guidance for 2026 - interim CEO Fred Vogt told analysts on the Q4 earnings call:

Right now, we're seeing remarkable growth in the Amtagvi business. We do need to be sure that our projections are well supported. So we're taking some time to do that. It's very early in the year right now, of course. But as I mentioned during the prepared remarks, we're going to be putting guidance out very, very soon. I think you'll see total product guidance. You'll see some guidance potentially on quarters. We'll have to see how our data is supported.

While Iovance stock jumped in value from ~$3 per share, to >$4 after earnings were reported, I'd call these results and updates decidedly mixed.

The lack of guidance would be a concern - in terms of both revenues, and expenses, given the somewhat precarious financial position the business is in - but the revenue growth - 30% sequentially, and the fact guidance was met and analysts earnings targets met or exceeded (in terms of revenues) can be considered positives, as can Wall Street's reaction to the updates.

Looking Ahead - Iovance Must Deliver On Key Catalysts In 2026

On or before April 10th, Replimune (REPL) expects to hear if its RP1 therapy indicated for advanced melanoma will be approved by the FDA, potentially placing it in direct competition with Amtagvi.

The drug was rejected for approval by the FDA last year, despite achieving an ORR of 32.9% in a 140-patient study, in combo with Bristol Myers Squibb's Opdivo (nivolumab), a checkpoint inhibitor, as the FDA ruled its pivotal study could not be "adequately interpreted due to the heterogeneity of the patient population".

Replimune stock surged by >100% when its resubmission was accepted in October last year, presumably reflecting the market's expectation the drug would secure approval at the second time of asking, although the current regime at the FDA are nothing if not unpredictable.

Personally, I would rather be in Iovance's position than Replimune's, although in its Q4 earnings press release, Replimune shares updated data as follows:

The analysis of acral melanoma data from the IGNYTE anti-PD-1 failed melanoma cohort showed treatment with RP1 combined with nivolumab resulted in an objective response rate ("ORR") of 44% (8/18) with a median duration of response of 11.9 months.

Replimune's candidate is an oncolytic virus, being an engineered strain of the herpes simplex virus, and doubts persist around the efficacy profile of the drug class - Pharma giant Merck shelved development of its oncolytic virus Cavatak in 2022, although Merck and Moderna (MRNA) have shared Phase 2b adjuvant data in relation to its "cancer vaccine", MRNA-4157, in melanoma patients, which showed that, in combination with KEYTRUDA it reduced the risk of recurrence or death by 49% compared to KEYTRUDA alone.

In summary, competition is likely to intensify in Amtagvi's only approved market if not in 2026, then certainly before 2030, which may make its billion dollar revenue target in the indication hard to achieve.

Attention therefore turns to the pipeline, illustrated below in a slide from Iovance's latest earnings presentation:

As we can see, lifileucel plus pembrolizumab - Merck's ~$30bn per annum selling cancer drug Keytruda - is the clinical study that is most advanced. Management provided the following update on its earnings call:

Remember, the trial design of TILVANCE is a comparison of the combination of pembro plus TIL versus pembro monotherapy. The trial is designed with 2 dual primary endpoints with one of them being ORR and the other one being the PFS. So ORR giving us an opportunity for an early read.

The benchmark in the KEYNOTE-006 (Keytruda monotherapy) trial for ORR was about in the mid-30s, probably the true real life ORR with the pembro monotherapy is probably more in the 30 and is slightly below percent range.

Remember, we have very encouraging data on the efficacy of the combination from our COM-202 cohort IA, which showed response rates up into the 60% range. So that's what's giving us the confidence around a successful readout for the ORR endpoint.

The study has apparently enrolled 670 patients, and interim data is likely in 2028, I'd speculate, so realistically, the therapy is unlikely to reach the market until 2030, if study data supports an approval submission, but the compelling efficacy data shared to date suggests the data may well be worth the wait.

Furthermore Iovance now has a Fast Track Designation from the FDA in relation to 2nd line nonsquamous NSCLC - an ORR of 25.6% has been demonstrated in an ongoing clinical study, with mDOR not reached at 25.4 months - while a PD-L1 negative, EGFR wild type cohort has shown an ORR of 64.3%, potentially supporting advancement in frontline NSCLC.

On the earnings call, regarding pre-treated NSCLC, the interim CEO told analysts:

We are rapidly advancing towards a supplemental biologics license application with a potential accelerated approval and launch in the second half of 2027.

This blockbuster opportunity is approximately 7x larger than our current melanoma opportunity, with 50,000 addressable patients for peak sales of $10 billion in the U.S. alone.

Our entire Amtagvi ATC network of U.S. academic and community practices can leverage their existing TIL infrastructure for rapid adoption in non-small cell lung cancer upon approval as well as future pipeline indications, such as sarcomas.

Iovance shared positive news around early sarcoma data on its earnings call also, revealing "an unprecedented 50% confirmed response rate", and for my money, it was in anticipation of these kinds of data readouts and developments that persuaded the market to value Iovance so highly back in 2020.

Iovance endured a difficult road to approval in its first, comparatively small indication, but real world data has supported that approval decision, and as the positive data rolls in for from other, larger indications, the next few years could - in my view - see Iovance's valuation finally rise to meet the market's once lofty expectations.

Concluding Thoughts - I'm Still Bullish, But I Am Also Cautious

People often say that reputations can take a lifetime to build, yet be destroyed in minutes, and it's important to stress that, despite the compelling (in my view) body of data that Iovance has built up over the years, one safety setback could seriously undermine lifileucel and therefore Iovance itself.

Although the company is developing next-generation TIL therapies, there's no question the vast majority of its valuation is tied up in lifileucel Amtagvi and there are still questions to be answered.

For example, can Amtagvi treat enough patients successfully to grow revenues at the rate the market demands (~30% per annum, I suspect)? Will its safety profile undermine its commercial appeal (remember, the drug has an "accelerated approval" only, its confirmatory study must deliver positive results or the drug could still be withdrawn from the market)?

Will Amtagvi soon face significant competition in its key market, will data in 1st line melanoma and NSCLC support successful approval submissions, and could the company's finances be exhausted before it secures any follow on approvals, leaving Iovance facing delisting / bankruptcy?

It's important investors are aware of these significant risks - and I would add to that it seems high-time Iovance appoints a permanent CEO - but despite these concerns, I remain long-term bullish on Iovance.

I am hopeful the company can secure an approval in NSCLC in 2027, and also in first line melanoma in time, and while I do not necessarily agree with management on the peak revenue opportunities, there's no question, in my view, that if these approvals are achieved, Iovance's share price will rise substantially in value.

I see upside potential of 200% - 300%, in fact, because I would expect the company to easily be able to meet blockbuster expectations by 2030 (if criteria above are met), and move much closer to profitability, and as I wrote in my last post, I would expect its valuation to be based around 3x price to sales, hence at least $3bn, and quite possibly more (witness the company's valuation back in 2020).

I believe data shared to date supports a bullish take on prospects for further approvals of lifileucel, and therefore, while I think the company may struggle to win over Wall Street in 2026, as I am not expecting guidance to significantly exceed the market's expectations, if long-term, goals are met, by 2030, it's not completely unrealistic to expect a >$10bn valuation given the huge market opportunities in play and the strength of the recent efficacy and safety data.

With that said, investors should be aware that setbacks, and sell-offs, are part and parcel of being an Iovance investor, my years of coverage suggest.

data.title: FLKR: South Korean Market Nosedives, Wait For The Right Entry Point
data.description:
The South Korean Kospi (KOSPI), one of the world’s hottest stock market indexes with a massive concentration in the top two companies, nosedived after reports of a prolonged war in the Middle East. The authorities are preparing to counter contingency risk as the worst selloff in decades could spillover to the broader market. Furthermore, the crash could spark a significant volatility in the global chips market. South Korea is ranked among the major global semiconductor powerhouses, with a dominating position in the memory chip market.

From an investment prospective, it is crucial to gauge whether the crash indicates a long-lasting downtrend or presenting a price correction to ease valuation. History shows that wars create attractive buying opportunities for investors. However, it is important to choose the right entry point. At present, the selloff appears to be in its early staged due to the expected war timeline of four to five weeks. Moreover, a valuation factor seems overheated because of a stunning AI-driven surge in the last twelve months. Therefore, I initiate coverage of Franklin FTSE South Korea ETF (FLKR) with a Hold rating.

A Review of South Korea Stock Market Crash

The South Korean stock market selloff is deepening as the Kospi plunged into a bear market after losing nearly 20% of its value in the last two days alone, marking the worst selloff since 2008. The intensity of selloff is massive as authorities are preparing to counter the spillover of equity market volatility into other markets. Lee Eog-weon, the South Korean Financial Services Commission Chairman, suggested concerned authorities to prepare contingency plans. During the Legoland credit crisis in 2022, South Korea initially formed a market stabilization fund of more than 50 trillion won to support financial markets.

The current stock market crash begin amid concerns over an impact of a prolonged war on energy supplies and prices. South Korea is among the largest importers of oil and gas from Gulf countries. The country is ranked among the top 10 oil consumers owing to its massive industrial infrastructure. The disruption in oil supplies and increase in prices can negatively impact the country’s inflation, interest rates, and economic growth.

Impact on Semiconductor Industry

South Korea is one of the largest exporters of semiconductor and memory chips. In January, its semiconductor exports rose 102% year over year to $20.54 billion, supported by a hyper demand for artificial intelligence servers. In February, its semiconductor exports soared by 160% to mark a new record high of $25.16 billion. Last year, the U.S. imported $13.8 billion of chips from South Korea, up 30% from the year-ago period. South Korean companies, such as Samsung and SK Hynix, are major players in the global memory chip market.

The U.S. chip stocks begin to feel the pressure on the South Korean chip market. Nvidia (NVDA), Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), Broadcom (AVGO), Qualcomm (QCOM) and other plunged sharply. iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX), which tracks the performance of NYSE Semiconductor Index, plunged 4% after an initial South Korean stock market crash. The higher production and transportation costs could negatively impact chip prices in months and quarters ahead.

Is it the Time to Invest in South Korean Securities?

Franklin FTSE South Korea ETF is one of the most popular investment vehicles to gain access to the South Korean large and mid-cap stocks. The fund tracks the performance of FTSE South Korea RIC Capped Index. In the last twelve months, FLKR’s share price rallied nearly 130%. In the latest crash, FLKR’s price plunged more than 12% from its late February peak.

Historical trends suggest that wars always create attractive buying opportunities. I believe the current selloff present a buying opportunity. However, the market trends and valuation indicates that the downtrend is in its early stages.

According to President Trump, the war could last four to five weeks. This war is crucial for global markets because it could significantly impact energy prices and global trade. Iran has closed the Strait of Hormuz, which makes up nearly a quarter of the total global oil supplies. The route is also critical for global liquefied natural gas exports. The majority of Gulf countries and Saudi Arabia have suspended their oil and gas supplies due to war.

This situation is adverse for South Korea because the country is sourcing almost 70% of its crude oil and 30% of natural gas from the Middle East.

Kang Sung-jin, economics professor at Korea University, anticipates that the impact of Middle East war on Korea could have significant economic and production consequences. He believes the war is expected disrupt and increase costs for both exports and imports. Similar to 1970 shocks, he believes a prolonged oil supply disruption could significantly boost the entire manufacturing goods cost structure.

On the other hand, FLKR also appears expensive based on its P/E ratio of 19, which is up from 14x at the beginning of year and around 10x its long term average. The fund’s price to book ratio also soared to 2.20 from 1.44 at the beginning of the year. FLKR’s high valuation factor is attributed to the stunning rally in the last twelve months. Overall, it is prudent to wait for the market to bottom out before initiating a new position.

FLKR Portfolio Review and Characteristics

Once the market hits a bottom, Franklin FTSE South Korea ETF could be an attractive investment vehicle, given its portfolio structure and strong characteristics. FLKR’s portfolio comprises large and mid caps. Unlike other ETFs, FLKR limits individual stock holding to a around 20% of its entire portfolio weight to improve the diversification factor and ensure the broader coverage of large and mid caps.

FLKR’s portfolio is spread across all eleven sectors, with a greater concentration in technology sector. The tech stocks account for almost half of its portfolio weight. The industrials, financials and consumer cyclical stocks also represent a healthy weight in its portfolio. Whereas, the health care, consumer defensive, communication, basic materials, energy and utilities sectors collectively represent nearly 14% of its entire portfolio weight.

From the technology sector, Samsung Electronics (SSNLF) and SK Hynix (HXSC.F) collectively make up 43% of its portfolio weight. FLKR also held more than 2.5% of its weight in Samsung Electronics preferred shares. Therefore, the fund’s performance is dominated by these two tech stocks.

In the last twelve months, these two stocks played a key role in FLKR’s blistering price surge. Samsung’s share price rallied more than 210% while SK Hynix shares soared 356% in the last twelve months. Both companies are dominant players in the global memory chip market. In the latest selloff, both stocks are down more than 20% from their recent peaks. Besides these two stocks, the rest of its top 10 stocks, including Hyundai Motor Co, SK Square Co, KB Financial Group and Kia Corporation, also plunged sharply in the current selloff. FLKR’s portfolio is composed of 159 stocks.

FLKR offers a dividend yield of 2.59%. It has lifted its dividends in the last three consecutive years. Its average dividend growth in the past three years was 37%. The fund offers a low expense ratio of 0.09% compared to the median of all ETFs of 0.50%. Its liquidity is also high with 281K in average shares trading volume compared to the median of all ETFs of 42K.

Final Thoughts

It is true that panic selling due to a war and valuation burst creates attractive buying opportunities for investors. However, choosing the right entry time is crucial to achieve hefty returns. At present, the South Korean stock market selloff is in it early stage with a significant downside intensity. Moreover, it is still unclear that how long the Middle East war will last. The uncertainty is also high about the real impact of war on global and Korean markets. Therefore, waiting for a better entry point seems to be a prudent strategy.

data.title: Winners & Losers Of REIT Earnings Season
data.description:
Winners & Losers of REIT Earnings Season

In our Earnings Season Recap, we analyze and highlight the incremental positive and negative takeaways for each property sector, noting the best and worst performers during this earnings season.

Over 200 U.S. REITs and homebuilders have reported fourth-quarter earnings results over the past six weeks, providing critical information on the state of the commercial and residential real estate industry. Overall, REIT earnings results were quite a bit better than consensus expectations - especially in the back-half of earnings season - and avoided the type of high-profile flops that overshadowed recent earnings seasons, with some impressive individual results across a balanced mix of sectors and market cap sizes. Of the 104 equity REITs that provided full-year guidance for Funds from Operations ("FFO"), 64 (62%) reported full-year 2025 FFO that topped the midpoint of their most recent guidance, 24 (23%) matched their guidance, while 16 (15%) missed their guidance - well above the historical average "beat rate" for the fourth quarter of 50-55%. Property-level metrics have posted more favorable trends, with roughly 70% of REITs beating their full-year Net Operating Income ("NOI") outlook - upside driven primarily by lower expenses - consistent with the "disinflation" theme observed in the back-half of 2025.

Before the Iran conflict sent energy prices to multi-year highs and lifted Treasury yields off of multi-year lows, REITs were enjoying a very favorable "goldilocks" macro data trends - cooling inflation alongside "good enough" labor market data - which helped the sector deliver notable earnings season outperformance over the S&P 500. Since the start of earnings season in mid-January, the Equity REIT Index (VNQ) is higher by 5.7%, outperforming the -0.7% decline from the S&P 500 during this time. Despite this recent outperformance, REITs still maintain a historically-wide 60-percentage-point underperformance gap versus the S&P since early 2022. Solid trends across most of the real estate sector come alongside a slightly weaker-than-expected set of earnings results across the broader U.S. equity market, particularly among the mega-cap tech giants that had shouldered the market gains in recent years. FactSet reports that 73% of S&P 500 constituents have topped consensus Earnings Per Share ("EPS") estimates - slightly below the 5-year average of 78% and the 10-year average of 76%.

Notable upside standouts this earnings season include Data Center, Senior Housing, Farmland, and Retail REITs. Senior Housing REITs - the perennial performance leader in recent years- reported another stellar quarter, with an equally impressive 2026 outlook for 15% NOI growth in their critical Senior Housing Operating Portfolio ("SHOP") segment. Data Center REITs continued to prove they could hang with the tech “big boys” amid the AI boom, maintaining near-zero vacancy and impressive pricing power. Results from Retail REIT across the strip center, regional mall, and single-tenant formats were a pleasant upside surprise, as limited new supply has kept occupancy rates near record-highs, and rent spreads in double-digits despite a slight demand moderation. Net Lease REITs, meanwhile, have accelerated their pace of acquisition activity as cap rates have ticked higher, resulting in increasingly attractive investment spreads. Speciality and agriculture-focused REIT sectors shined in the back-half of earnings season: Farmland and Cannabis REITs showed signs of operator stabilization after a post-pandemic slump in crop prices. Billboard REITs showed no signs of cooling in demand for Out-of-Home advertising, while Cold Storage REITs halted their cold streak of quarterly "misses" with a decent quarter. Hotel REITs also posted surprisingly solid results despite a soft stretch of travel demand in late 2025. Manufactured Housing REITs were the long residential sector to earn a spot among the "winners", with impressive performance in their core MH segment.

This season’s leaderboard is topped by REITs that agreed to deals to be taken private, reinforcing the “REIT Exodus” trend that has persisted over the past several quarters. Small-cap net lease REIT Peakstone (PKST) has surged near 50% after it agreed to be acquired by Brookfield Asset Management in an all-cash deal at $21/share - a 34% premium to its last closing price. PKST owns 70 industrial properties across 18 states, with a focus on the Industrial Outdoor Storage ("IOS") segment - a niche category consisting of outdoor yards used to store large equipment and materials. Homebuilder Tri Pointe (TPH) has jumped nearly 40% after it agreed to be acquired by Japan's Sumitomo Forestry for in an all-cash $4.5B deal at $47/share - a 29% premium to its prior closing price. Veris Residential (VRE) – a small-cap NYC-metro focused apartment REIT that owns roughly 6,500 units across 17 properties – has rallied nearly 30% after reaching a deal to be taken private by an investor consortium led by Affinius Capital in an all-cash transaction valued at $19.00 per share. Since 2022, a total of 42 REITs have been acquired, liquidated, or formally put up for sale, as deep and persistent public-market NAV discounts relative to private valuations continue to incentivize boards and management teams to pursue strategic alternatives aimed at narrowing this valuation gap and maximizing shareholder value.

While there weren't any major bombshells, we have seen a handful of disappointing results and softer-than-expected trends across a handful of sectors. Results from Office REITs showed that while property-level trends have inflected positively—evidenced by improved leasing volumes, modest occupancy gains, and stabilizing in-place rents—bottom-line profitability remains constrained by elevated leverage, higher interest expense, and balance sheets still healing from years of value erosion. Results from Residential REITs - both apartment and single-family rental - were also modestly disappointing, with results showing the early-year reacceleration in rent growth completely reversed in the back half of the year, even in the tight Northeast and Midwest markets. Results from Commercial Mortgage REIT were a setback after several encouraging quarters, as pockets of distress lingered in office, multifamily, and lab space loans, stretching dividend coverage and prompting a pair of cuts. For Residential Mortgage REITs, however, the underlying earnings were quite encouraging, highlighted by an unexpected 20% dividend increase from a name that's already paying a double-digit dividend yield. Results from Cell Tower REITs were relatively soft, as expected, given the DISH default and ongoing carrier consolidation. Industrial REITs showed that fundamentals remain steady outside of the still-struggling West Coast, but the upward inflection in fundamentals isn't expected until late 2026 amid lingering oversupply. Results from Medical Office REITs were a step in the right direction, with upside surprises from the core MOB segment offset by lab space weakness.

Winners of REIT Earnings Season

Winner #1: Senior Housing: (Final Grade: A)

Positives: Stellar senior housing outlook for 2026 for 15%+ NOI growth after 15-20% growth in 2025; External growth activity broadening beyond WELL; Skilled nursing tenant rent coverage improves to decade-highs.

Negatives: Low-single-digit NOI growth in non-SHOP segments; Slight FFO miss from CTRE on equity raise (but still impressive double-digit growth); Mixed results from NHI & LTC amid continued push into SHOP segment.

Big Winners: American Healthcare (AHR), Welltower (WELL)

Big Losers: None

Winner #2: Data Center: (Final Grade: A)

Positives: Impressive 2026 FFO outlook for double-digit growth; resilient pricing power despite booming investment in capacity; EQIX 10% dividend hike; DLR has its "mojo" back after a half-decade of zero growth.

Negatives: Choppy hypersale leasing volume (primarily on capacity constraints), FX headwinds on recent dollar strength; Fermi has tumbled since its IPO after the anchor tenant backed out of Project Matador.

Big Winners: Equinix (EQIX), Iron Mountain (IRM)

Big Losers: Fermi (FRMI)

Winner #3: Net Lease: (Final Grade: A)

Positives: Sector-leading 10 FFO beats with no misses; Rebound in acquisitions as investment spreads become more accretive, No major new credit issues; Strength from value-oriented retail, automotive, experiential.

Negatives: Typical mix of tenant-specific issues in softer segments (pharmacy, restaurants, theater); Softer growth rates in 2025 & 2026 from the sector's "big boys" (O, NNN) as size makes it harder to move the needle.

Big Winners: Peakstone (PKST), Curbline (CURB), Postal Realty (PSTL)

Big Losers: Generation Income (GIPR)

Winner #4: Billboard: (Final Grade: A-)

Positives: Rebound in ad spending among national advertisers, Big outperformance in transit segment (led by New York MTA), Continued benefit from digital billboard conversions, World Cup & political tailwinds in 2026.

Negatives: Tough comps from political spending last year; Other weaker segments: telecom, wine/beer, government, and automotive; Core billboard segment (non-transit) was softer in 2025.

Big Winners: Outfront (OUT), Lamar (LAMR)

Big Losers: None

Winner #5: Regional Mall: (Final Grade: A-)

Positives: Upper-end consumer remains strong and still spending; Tanger's record-high leasing volumes & occupancy rate; Simon's 5% domestic NOI growth with same 2026 outlook; MAC beginning to get its act together.

Negatives: Announced retail closures in recent months have been more mall-heavy relative to recent trends; CBL's 2026 FFO forecast suggests it's not out of the woods yet; Lower-end consumer cited as segment of softness.

Big Winners: Tanger (SKT), Simon (SPG)

Big Losers: None

Winner #6: Farmland: (Final Grade: A-)

Positives: Strong harvest & fewer drought conditions help farmers after several tough seasons; Impressive rebound from FPI since 2023; FPI's 10% dividend hike; LAND stabilizing via re-leasing and direct farming operations.

Negatives: Grain prices still near four-year lows & hovering around pre-pandemic levels; Lingering farmland vacancies; Tariff & trade uncertainty.

Big Winners: Gladstone Land (LAND), Farmland Partners (FPI)

Big Losers: None

Winner #7: Strip Centers: (Final Grade: A-)

Positives: Double-digit leasing spreads for 15th-straight quarter; Occupancy rates still near record-highs; small-shop occupancy improving; Handful of dividend hikes, including UE (+11%) and IVT (+5%).

Negatives: Still relatively quiet on the acquisitions front (leaning heavily into redevelopment); Noisy report from AKR on one-time factors; some short-term occupancy headwinds from recent bankruptcies (Rite Aid).

Big Winners: Brixmor (BRX), Kite Realty (KRG),

Big Losers: Site Centers (SITC)

Winner #8: Casino: (Final Grade: B+)

Positives: GLPI forecasting 2026 will be the strongest year of growth since 2019; Steady FFO beats driven by mid-sized M&A deals and development financing (VICI buys Golden Entertainment, GLPI buys Bally's Lincoln).

Negatives: Lingering concerns over Las Vegas weakness (visitor traffic down 5-8% y/y); Key operators struggling (CZR, PENN); Vegas weakness due to steep dip in inbound international tourism (Canada especially).

Big Winner: Gaming & Leisure Properties (GLPI)

Big Loser: None

Winner #9: Hotel (Final Grade: B+)

Positives: Upside surprises: 9 of 10 hotel REITs reported 2025 FFO growth above prior guidance (+1.5% average vs. -3.2% expected); Group demand remained the standout, particularly in upscale urban / resort portfolios.

Negatives: Tone for 2026 turned more cautious: five REITs guide to negative FFO growth; Lingering margin and cost pressures; Economy segment remains soft; International travel remains weak & Middle East conflict won't help.

Big Winners: Chatham Lodging (CLDT), Diamondrock (DRH)

Big Losers: Braemar Hotels (BHR), Ashford Hospitality (AHT)

Winner #10: Manufactured Housing: (Final Grade: B+)

Positives: Core manufactured housing portfolio continues to outperform; Solid mid-single digit rent increases expected in 2026; Sun getting its "mojo" back as U.K. portfolio stabilizes; Dividend hikes from SUI (8%) and ELS (5%).

Negatives: Miss from UMH on equity issuance; RV segment remains weak - especially from Canadians; Still quiet on the domestic M&A front; Softness in lower-end consumer will weigh on ability to push rents.

Big Winner: Sun Communities (SUI)

Big Loser: UMH Properties (UMH)

REIT Earnings Season Losers

Loser#1: Office: (Final Grade: C)

Negatives: Balance sheet repairs pushing the upward FFO inflection in 2027 despite improved property-level trends; No bottoming yet in California market; Mamdani's property tax proposal hit NYC-focused names (SLG, BXP ESRT).

Positives: Best quarter of leasing volume since mid-2010s; Occupancy rates are finally bottoming; Continued Sunbelt outperformance (CUZ, HIW); Solid government/defense leasing activity as DOGE concerns fade.

Big Winners: COPT Defense (CDP), Easterly Government (DEA)

Big Losers: Kilroy (KRC), SL Green (SLG), Hudson Pacific (HPP)

Loser #2: Commercial Mortgage REITs (Final Grade: C)

Negatives: Worst BVPS decline since 2021; Loan downgrades in multifamily, lab space, and office; Stretched dividend coverage prompts FBRT dividend cut & KREF to "review policy;" No bottom yet for struggling CMTG or GPMT.

Positives: ABR shows signs of stabilization after rough 2025, ARI's acquisition by Athene signals some M&A appetite & confirms deep NAV discounts vs. private markets, Solid quarter from the sector's "big boys" (STWD & BXMT).

Big Winners: Arbor Realty (ABR), Apollo Commercial (ARI)

Big Losers: Granite Point (GPMT), Claros Mortgage (CMTG)

Loser #3: Apartment: (Final Grade: C+)

Negatives: New lease rent trends (-5.2%) worsen after rebound in early 2026, Softness spreads from Sunbelt to Mid-Atlantic and West Coast, Early signs of immigration impact on demand (especially at lower-tier).

Positives: Renewal rent growth (+3.5%) still holding up, Favorable expense control trends (moderate property tax and insurance increases), Veris Residential gets bought out (and others - CSR, ELME - still in play).

Big Winners: Veris Residential (VRE)

Big Losers: Elme Communities (ELME), Clipper Realty (CLPR)

Loser #4: Cell Tower: (Final Grade: C+)

Negatives: Material hit from DISH/Echostar default with pending legal fight; 3rd-straight year of low-to-negative FFO growth expected in 2026; No near-term catalysts to drive upward inflection amid ongoing carrier consolidation.

Positives: SBAC's 13% dividend hike; Guidance beats on cost savings with more efficiencies planned; AMT's data center outperformance; Chatter on 6G networks helps lift sentiment; Valuations hovering around historic lows.

Big Winners: SBA Communications (SBAC), American Tower (AMT)

Big Losers: None

Loser #5: Single Family Rental: (Final Grade: B-)

Negatives: Policy risk remains key overhang as White House advances the institutional homebuying ban; Deceleration in new lease rates across all regions; Sunbelt rental trends remain especially weak.

Positives: Favorable expense controls (modest insurance and property tax increases); Continued push into Built-to-Rent internal development to offset acquisitions risk; Renewals remain buoyant; AMH dividend hike (10%).

Big Winners: None

Big Losers: American Homes (AMH)

Loser #6: Self-Storage REITs: (Final Grade: B)

Negatives: Occupancy rates remain under pressure; Home moving rates (which drives storage demand) still historically low; Still dealing with pockets of oversupply (especially in Sunbelt) after COVID-era development boom.

Positives: New lease rates ("Street Rates") finally stabilized after 12 quarters of declines; Favorable expense controls (modest advertising, labor, and property taxes); Signs of recovery from NSA after rough 2022-2024.

Big Winners: Public Storage (PSA), CubeSmart (CUBE)

Big Losers: SmartStop (SMA), Global Self Storage (SELF)

Loser #7: Medical Office / Lab Space:(Final Grade: B)

Negatives: Lab space fundamentals remain very soft with positive inflection not expected until 2027 at the earliest; Disappointing 2026 FFO outlook given the healthy MOB property-level trends; Misses from XRN and SILA.

Positives: Solid Medical Office Building ("MOB") leasing volume & rent spreads; MOB guidance sees 2-3% Same-Store NOI growth; Signs of stabilization at MPT after brutal stretch from 2022-2025.

Big Winners: Universal Health (UHT), Medical Properties (MPT)

Big Losers: Alexandria (ARE), Chiron / Global Medical (XRN)

Loser #8: Industrial: (Final Grade: B)

Negatives: Market rent growth still negative (-4% Y/Y) with inflection not expected until 2H26, Setback in sluggish SoCal market; Smaller tenants still delaying major lease decisions, PLYM's sale at a discount to prior offer.

Positives: Reacceleration in leasing volume and rent spreads in "middle-America" markets following tariff-related pause; Tenant recognition of the need for supply chain diversification.

Big Winners: Prologis (PLD), First Industrial (FR)

Big Losers: LXP Industrial (LXP), Rexford (REXR)

Loser #9: Residential Mortgage REITs (Final Grade: B)

Positives: Much-improved dividend coverage after two-straight quarters of increased Book Values (+2.0%) & EPS (+3.8%) increases; Agency mREITs report +5% average BVPS increase and +2-4% QTD; CIM's dividend hike.

Negatives: Relative weakness from long-outperforming MSR segment, highly competitive refinancing environment for services-heavy mREITs (PMT, RITM); TWO back at pre-acquisition value on UWMC share price slump.

Big Winners: Chimera (CIM), Redwood (RWT), MFA Financial (MFA)

Big Losers: Two Harbors (TWO), Rithm Capital (RITM)

Loser #10: Cold Storage (Final Grade: B)

Negatives: Both REITs see double-digit FFO dip in 2026 (as expected); Not out of the woods yet in pressure on both pricing and economic occupancy as customers continue to manage inventory tightly.

Positives: Stabilization in occupancy rates; Strong harvest season helping; Finally a "beat" after several downbeat quarters; New leasing volume has been a bright spot, offset by reductions in the base business and churn.

Big Winner: Lineage (LINE)

Big Loser: None

Earnings Recap: Strong Results, But Will It Matter?

Overall, REIT earnings results were quite a bit better than consensus expectations - especially in the back-half of earnings season - and avoided the type of high-profile flops that overshadowed recent earnings seasons, with some impressive individual results across a balanced mix of sectors and market cap sizes. Upside standouts this earnings season include Senior Housing, Data Center, and Retail REITs. Speciality and agriculture-focused REIT sectors shined in the back-half of earnings season. There were no major bombshells this earnings season, but Apartment, Office, and Commercial Mortgage REITs have been the notable laggards. Multifamily rents softened in late 2025, particularly in the Sunbelt. Office REIT results showed progress at the property-level, but lingering headwinds on bottom-line FFO from balance sheet repair. Two REITs accepted takeover offers - surging more than 30% as a result - indicating that the public market discount is as wide as ever, and the "REIT Exodus" trend will persist until public REITs can close this valuation gap. And while these REITs should be commended for pursuing the path that maximizes shareholder value, investors are seeing the investible universe shrink and more assets pulled out of the public markets - an unfortunate byproduct of REITs being "too cheap" for too long.
